topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the edge of his desk with a trembling finger, the cheap laminate feeling slick beneath his touch. The documents spread before him—a prospectus, a valuation report, a chillingly optimistic projection of future mergers. Every line seemed to mock him. He reread the section detailing the acquisition strategy, his breath catching in his throat with each word. The fluorescent lights of the open-plan office seemed suddenly harsh, exposing every corner of the room to his panicked scrutiny. He looked up, scanning the faces of his colleagues, searching for any sign of shared dread, but they were all engrossed in their screens, oblivious to the seismic shift occurring beneath their feet. A cold knot tightened in his stomach, constricting his chest until drawing a full breath felt like an impossible effort. He slowly pushed the papers away, the sound of the sliding cardstock echoing unnervingly loud in the silence of his cubicle.

[story 2]

"This can't be right," Sarah whispered, the sound barely audible over the hum of the server room. She stared at the signed letter, her knuckles white where she gripped the edge of the conference table. The numbers on the spreadsheet were a grotesque caricature of their hard-won success; the projected figures suggested not growth, but complete dissolution. A wave of nausea washed over Mark, making his vision swim slightly. He felt a sudden, intense pressure behind his eyes, as if something heavy was pressing down on his skull. He stood up abruptly, knocking his chair slightly askew, the scrape of metal on tile sounding deafening in the sudden stillness. He couldn't look at anyone; the reality of the situation felt too vast, too final, and his hands began to shake uncontrollably as he tried to steady himself.

[story 3]

I found the file tucked away in the dusty archive, a document labeled simply 'Future.' My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that drowned out the ambient noise of the office. When I finally understood the terms—the sale, the handover, the complete erasure of everything we had built—a strange numbness settled over me, quickly followed by a sharp, icy dread. I sat back down, my gaze fixed on the faded ink, unable to process the implications. My mouth went dry, and I had to consciously force myself to swallow, the action feeling monumental. I could feel the blood draining from my extremities, leaving me feeling strangely lightheaded, as if the ground beneath me had suddenly given way.
